title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 13 (Fragment 2)
text:
“I should close the gate, if I were you,” said Chrestomanci.

Paolo looked up at him and wondered why people looked so different in uniform. He pulled himself together and dragged the two halves of the gate shut. Chrestomanci helped him slot the big iron bars in to lock it. As he did, he said, “I was at the Casa Petrocchi around dawn, so there’s no great need for explanations. But I would like to know what’s the matter in the kitchen this time.”

Paolo looked. Eight baskets piled high with round tan-colored loaves stood outside the kitchen. There were agitated noises from inside it, and a curious long droning sound. “I think it’s Lucia’s spell again,” he said.

He and Chrestomanci set off across the yard. Before they had gone three steps, the aunts burst out of the kitchen and rushed towards him. Antonio and the uncles hurried down from the gallery, and cousins arrived from everywhere else. Aunt Francesca surged out of the Saloon. She had spent the night there, and looked as if she had. Chrestomanci was soon in the middle of a crowd and holding several conversations at once.

“You were quite right to call me,” he said to Rosa, and to Aunt Francesca, “Old Niccolo is good for years yet, but you should rest.” To Elizabeth and Antonio, he said, “I know about Tonino,” and to Rinaldo, “This is my fourth uniform today. There’s heavy fighting in the hills and I had to get through somehow. What possessed the Duke,” he asked the uncles, “to declare war so soon? I could have got help from Rome if he’d waited.” None of them knew, and they all told him so at once. “I know,” said Chrestomanci. “I know. No war-spells. I think our enemy enchanter has made a mistake over Tonino and Angelica. If it does nothing else, it allows me a free hand.” Then, as the clamor showed no sign of abating, he said, “By the way, the Final Reserve has been called up,” and nodded to Paolo to give the paper to Antonio.

In the sober hush that this produced, Chrestomanci pushed his way to the kitchen and put his head inside. “My goodness me!” Paolo heard him murmur.

Paolo ducked under all the people crowding round Antonio and looked into the kitchen under Chrestomanci’s elbow. He looked into a wall of insects. The place was black with them, and glittering, and crawling, and dense with different humming. Flies of all kinds, mosquitoes, wasps and midges filled the airspace. Beetles, ants, moths and a hundred other crawling things occupied the floor and shelves and sink. Peering through the buzzing clouds, Paolo was almost sure he saw a swarm of locusts on the cooking stove. It was even worse than he had imagined the Petrocchi kitchen when he was little.

Chrestomanci drew a deep breath. Paolo suspected he was trying not to laugh. They both looked around for Lucia, who was standing on one leg among the breadbaskets, wondering whether to run away. “I am sure,” Chrestomanci said to her—he was trying not to laugh; he had to start again. “I am sure people have talked to you about misusing spells. But—just out of interest—what did you use?”

“She used her own words to the Angel of Caprona !” Aunt Maria said, bursting angrily out of the crowd. “Gina’s nearly out of her mind!”

“All the children did it,” Lucia said defiantly. “It wasn’t only me.”

Chrestomanci looked at Paolo, and Paolo nodded. “A considerable tribute to the powers of the younger Montanas,” said Chrestomanci. He turned and snapped his fingers into the buzzing, crawling kitchen. Not much happened. The air cleared enough for Paolo to see that it was indeed locusts on the cooker, but that was all. Chrestomanci’s eyebrows went up a little. He tried again. This time nothing happened at all. He retreated from the buzzing, looking thoughtful.

vocabulary.definition: Making a continuous, low, vibrating sound like that of bees or insects.
vocabulary.example: Peering through the buzzing clouds, Paolo was almost sure he saw a swarm of locusts on the cooking stove.

vocabulary.definition: A proper noun used in the text, referring to a character's name.
vocabulary.example: “I should close the gate, if I were you,” said Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: Informal spoken exchanges between two or more people.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci was soon in the middle of a crowd and holding several conversations at once.

vocabulary.definition: A large number of people gathered closely together.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci was soon in the middle of a crowd and holding several conversations at once.

vocabulary.definition: The first appearance of light in the morning before sunrise.
vocabulary.example: As he did, he said, “I was at the Casa Petrocchi around dawn, so there’s no great need for explanations.

vocabulary.definition: To announce something officially or publicly.
vocabulary.example: What possessed the Duke,” he asked the uncles, “to declare war so soon?

vocabulary.definition: In a manner showing resistance or bold disobedience.
vocabulary.example:
”

“All the children did it,” Lucia said defiantly.

vocabulary.definition: A person who uses magic or enchantments.
vocabulary.example: I think our enemy enchanter has made a mistake over Tonino and Angelica.

vocabulary.definition: A person or group that opposes or attacks another.
vocabulary.example: I think our enemy enchanter has made a mistake over Tonino and Angelica.

vocabulary.definition: A hinged barrier used to close an opening in a fence or wall.
vocabulary.example: “I should close the gate, if I were you,” said Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: A strong necessity or requirement.
vocabulary.example: As he did, he said, “I was at the Casa Petrocchi around dawn, so there’s no great need for explanations.

vocabulary.definition: A silence or stillness, often suddenly imposed.
vocabulary.example: In the sober hush that this produced, Chrestomanci pushed his way to the kitchen and put his head inside.

vocabulary.definition: A small arthropod animal with six legs and generally one or two pairs of wings.
vocabulary.example: He looked into a wall of insects.

vocabulary.definition: A strong, hard magnetic silvery-gray metal.
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci helped him slot the big iron bars in to lock it.

vocabulary.definition: To speak or say something in a low, soft, or indistinct voice.
vocabulary.example: ” Paolo heard him murmur.

vocabulary.definition: Abilities or capacities to do something or act in a particular way.
vocabulary.example: “A considerable tribute to the powers of the younger Montanas,” said Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: To withdraw, especially from enemy forces or a difficult situation.
vocabulary.example: He retreated from the buzzing, looking thoughtful.

vocabulary.definition: To move with urgency or rapidity.
vocabulary.example: Before they had gone three steps, the aunts burst out of the kitchen and rushed towards him.

vocabulary.definition: A large room or hall for social gatherings.
vocabulary.example: Aunt Francesca surged out of the Saloon.

vocabulary.definition: Marked by seriousness, gravity, or solemnity; not drunk.
vocabulary.example: In the sober hush that this produced, Chrestomanci pushed his way to the kitchen and put his head inside.

vocabulary.definition: A word or phrase believed to have magical power.
vocabulary.example: “I think it’s Lucia’s spell again,” he said.

vocabulary.definition: To rise or increase suddenly and powerfully.
vocabulary.example: Aunt Francesca surged out of the Saloon.

vocabulary.definition: A large or dense group of insects moving together.
vocabulary.example: Peering through the buzzing clouds, Paolo was almost sure he saw a swarm of locusts on the cooking stove.

vocabulary.definition: An act, statement, or gift showing respect or admiration.
vocabulary.example: “A considerable tribute to the powers of the younger Montanas,” said Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: Distinctive clothing worn by members of the same organization or group.
vocabulary.example: Paolo looked up at him and wondered why people looked so different in uniform.
